data.title: lululemon athletica: Activist Interest Reinforces A Mispriced Turnaround Story
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

lululemon athletica (LULU) is down nearly 50% over the past year, trading at some of the lowest levels in over half a decade, fueled by rising internal and macro uncertainty, which I believe offers a solid opportunity to get exposure to what could end up being a very strong turnaround, which recently sparked the attention of legendary names in the market.

Reaching the end of their 5-year cycle soon, LULU outlined $11 billion in revenue for 2025 (nearly tripling during this cycle despite the ongoing environment) during their Q3 Earnings Call, with significant growth internationally despite the domestic weakness, fueled by China and their digital expansion.

Recently, thanks to a strong holiday season, LULU even put out a statement saying that they now expect the net revenue and EPS to be toward the high end of the previously guided $3.5 billion to $3.585 billion and $4.66 to $4.76, respectively.

Although the free cash flow has been negative over 9M'25 so far due to significant working capital hits combined with an increase in CAPEX, we should note the significant seasonality (see below), with Q4 often coming with very significant operating cash flows.

As we can see, over half of 2024's cash flow from operating activities came in the last quarter, with the free cash flow reaching $1.58 billion, $1.64 billion in 2024 and 2023, while 2022 was hit by their significant inventory build-up and other working capital issues.

Still, even if we assume a decrease to $1.5 billion or even $1.4 billion as a result of the higher costs, tariffs, and bigger CAPEX, that's still not bad at all for a ~$23.68 billion market cap, especially from a high-quality business with strong growth potential.

Financially, based on LULU's latest report, we can see a very solid position, with the current assets alone more than covering their total liabilities, with over $1 billion in cash and equivalents (and ~$2 billion in inventory) that places them in a solid position to fund their international expansion.

If they want to, there is plenty of room to fuel this through debt, especially if they tap into international markets and manage to get it for lower yields.

As of their Q3'25 report, LULU has repurchased ~$906.58 million worth of stock during 9M'25, which is unfortunately a decrease compared to 9M'24, when the stock was significantly more expensive. Still, on December 3, 2025, LULU's board authorized a $1 billion increase to their buybacks, bringing the remaining authorization to $1.6 billion now, and I'd argue that this is a solid time for accelerated buybacks, especially since they can afford them.

Risks & Opportunities

Although debt costs aren't that important for LULU directly since they have a meaningless amount, they can still affect their consumers, partners, and especially the market's expectations, as they are tied to the risk-free rate that's ultimately impacting valuations.

I believe it's important to mention that we're not necessarily talking about a company-specific issue (Nike (NKE) reported stagnating numbers for a few years now) or the argument that LULU has been just a fad that's now fading.

The recent 33% growth in international revenue is a good argument against the latter, while looking at many other companies (even well beyond just apparel), we can see that we're actually talking about country-wide issues, as even food companies that I've covered recently are reporting significant slowdowns in volumes, with a general de-premiumization trend and accentuated K-shaped economic developments.

Countless recent studies showed the weakness and stress reported by consumers, as they are facing rising costs of living, lower income, job insecurity, etc., and LULU is far from immune to that, having to maintain/grow their brand while also facing competitive pressure from industry titans on top.

This has been going on especially since the rate hikes, but the tariff uncertainty adds another layer to it, while the uncertainty around the Fed's independence and its next moves can lead to even more pressure. To that, add all the rising geopolitical uncertainty (Greenland, Iran, Venezuela), renewed tariff uncertainty as Trump just threatened that he "may punish countries with tariffs" if they don't agree with his Greenland plans, and other factors that can lead to even less confidence from the market.

As a result, the market can push debt costs higher, which is its way of adjusting the reward to the new risks and uncertainty it has to take compared to the "safe haven" that US treasuries used to be, while the environment continues to remain uncertain, with hard, safe haven assets like gold or silver reaching new all-time highs almost on a daily basis nowadays. Again, it's not as important for LULU from a balance sheet perspective, but it can influence their growth and valuation.

Still, given the high quality and brand popularity combined with the massive expansion potential, I wouldn't be surprised to see a giant like LVMH (LVMHF) try to buy LULU at some point if this pressure continues.

These kinds of acquisitions are at the core of industry titans like LVMH, who can use their premium valuations to buy strong brands and develop them considerably using their global supply chain and know-how, which can arguably unlock significant value in LULU's international expansion and potentially be very accretive for LVMH, especially if they buy them at a distressed valuation.

Meanwhile, the recent pressure from their founder and even a recent $1 billion position from Elliott Investment Management can also lead to positive changes, and the market seems to believe that despite growing above peers recently, LULU could've achieved more than a 7% increase in revenue if they had better leadership.

The CEO is already stepping down (resignation effective at the end of January 2026), and this is generally the kind of position where Elliott plays very well, allowing them to even reshape the company's future thanks to the kind of influence they have, already viewing the former CFO and COO of Ralph Lauren (RL), Jane Nielsen - where she had an "immense success"—as a strong candidate.

In other words, right now, we're looking at a ton of interest not only from an industry veteran that built one of the biggest apparel brands, but also one of the most popular activist investor funds, and I believe that should say something about the company's potential.

Meanwhile, LULU has recently announced their plans to continue scaling their international expansion, preparing to enter Greece, Austria, Poland, Hungary, and Romania in 2026 in addition to the previously announced entry into India.

Risks Reiterated

Despite the strong financial position and significant international expansion potential, LULU is still ultimately limited by the consumer environment, especially since we're talking about a high-quality brand that can be pressured by de-premiumization trends.

Meanwhile, we should keep in mind that an international expansion can also fail, especially if they get "lost" in the management/leadership changing process, while their domestic popularity can fade as competition intensifies, with industry giants potentially taking advantage of any missteps.

Valuation

For a Lululemon's FCF to Equity valuation, let's begin with $1.4 billion in free cash flow in 2025, which is below the past couple of years and takes into account macro pressures, followed by a 7.5% CAGR thanks to low single-digit growth in the US combined with double-digit international expansion, followed by a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a 10% discount rate to take into account the higher risks compared to the industry's giants and 118.55 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $24.71 billion, for an intrinsic value of $208.46 per share, which is above the current $201.87 at the moment of writing this.

Even this relatively conservative valuation (low starting FCF, much lower CAGR than recent history even during a weak environment, higher discount rate) leads to a price that's slightly above the current level, while there is plenty of potential for them to do much better thanks to tariffs, improving consumer environment, and their international expansion.

Conclusion

Overall, Lululemon is a buy in my opinion, with strong cash flow, a healthy balance sheet, high-quality products, and significant potential thanks to their international expansion.

There are definitely risks, although the market seems to be accounting for quite a weak future already, as if they were already in a much more mature stage, but the industry's recovery combined with all the pressure on the board and leadership could lead to a very profitable turnaround story.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TPZ: Why Energy Investors Should Not Only Chase Dividends
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Tortoise Electrification Infrastructure ETF (NYSE:TPZ) is displaying concerning signals for its top holdings including significant leverage, low growth, and other risk factors. While the fund offers a solid dividend yield, investors should not overlook these factors along with a high expense ratio that will erode returns. As a result, the fund warrants a sell rating, particularly for investors that are seeking a low-cost fund for capital appreciation in addition to income.

Tortoise Electrification Infrastructure ETF – Overview and Compared ETFs

TPZ is an actively managed ETF that seeks to track energy and electrification companies with the objective of monthly income. The fund accomplishes this objective by capturing dividend-paying companies and using an options strategy. With its inception in 2024, the fund has 33 holdings and $131M in AUM. TPZ includes 64% weight on power infrastructure along with 30% weight on natural gas infrastructure and 7% weight on liquids infrastructure.

For comparison purposes, other funds examined are First Trust NASDAQ Clean Edge Smart Grid Infrastructure Index Fund (GRID), Tema Electrification ETF (VOLT), and iShares Global Infrastructure Fund (IGF). GRID seeks to capture companies in the electric energy infrastructure business including electric grid, electric meters, and energy storage. I wrote previously on GRID two years ago and rated the fund a buy. VOLT seeks to capture companies that will benefit from increased electricity demand including smart grid technology companies. IGF differs from TPZ in that it is a global fund focusing on multiple types of infrastructure including electricity, communication, and transportation.

Funds Compared: Performance, Fees, and Dividend Yields

Because TPZ was initiated in 2024, the fund does not have a lengthy history to compare performance. However, over the past year TPZ has significantly underperformed peer energy funds. For reasons discussed in this article, I believe this trend will continue through 2026 and beyond.

A key drawback for TPZ is its high expense ratio. While the fund offers a significant dividend yield at almost 4%, this yield is eaten away by high fees. Therefore, although the fund has a noteworthy income component, the combination of a high expense ratio with low capital appreciation must also be considered.

Expense Ratio, AUM, and Dividend Yield Comparison

TPZ

GRID

VOLT

IGF

Expense Ratio

0.85%

0.56%

0.75%

0.39%

AUM

$131.05M

$5.68B

$293.50M

$9.11B

Dividend Yield TTM

3.92%

0.97%

0.42%

3.18%

Dividend Growth 3 YR CAGR

N/A

11.97%

N/A

17.41%

Source: Seeking Alpha, 17 Jan 26

TPZ Holdings and Key Consideration Factors

TPZ is dominated by its concentration on midstream energy, or companies that offer services between production and end-use. These midstream companies include Energy Transfer (ET), Etergy Corporation (ETR), and the Williams Companies (WMB). The ETF also is heavy on power generation companies like Constellation Energy (CEG) and NRG Energy (NRG).

Top 10 Holdings for TPZ and Peer Energy Infrastructure Funds

TPZ – 33 holdings

GRID – 113 holdings

VOLT – 32 holdings

IGF – 76 holdings

CEG – 7.04%

SWX:ABBN – 8.15%

POWL – 6.45%

AENA – 5.11%

ET – 6.57%

LON:NG – 8.09%

BELFB – 6.42%

NEE – 5.02%

VST – 5.47%

EPA:SU – 7.78%

NEE – 6.30%

TCL – 4.84%

ETR – 5.42%

ETN – 7.59%

APH – 4.99%

ENB – 3.93%

SRE – 5.42%

JCI – 7.51%

PWR – 4.56%

PAC – 3.87%

TRP – 5.01%

ETR:EOAN – 4.33%

HUBB – 4.20%

IBE – 3.86%

EVRG – 4.93%

BIT:PRY – 4.24%

AEP – 4.05%

AIA – 3.00%

CWEN – 4.91%

PWR – 3.84%

ETR – 3.96%

CEG – 2.90%

NRG – 4.89%

HUBB – 2.96%

ET – 3.94%

SO – 2.87%

WMB – 4.87%

NVT – 2.04%

IDA – 3.81%

WMB – 2.80%

Source: Multiple, compiled by author on 17 Jan 26

Ultimately, TPZ is a highly concentrated fund with almost 60% weight on its top 10 holdings. The fund’s top three holdings alone make up almost 20% weight and are also indicative of TPZ’s active management strategy. While many of the fund’s top holdings offer a strong dividend yield, they also have issues that will lead to limited growth. These factors are discussed below for TPZ’s top three holdings.

Constellation Energy: Supply of Uranium Raises Concern

TPZ’s top holding, CEG, at over 7% weight, represents the first concerning factor for the fund. Constellation Energy’s key competitive advantage is that it provides the largest network of nuclear facilities in the United States. However, Constellation Energy faces unique supply challenges. CEG’s facilities require not just uranium but the conversion of uranium to gas and enriching that gas to fissile U-235 for fuel rods. Currently, the world’s largest producer of uranium is Kazakhstan, a Central Asian nation that has grown closer to China in recent years. Partly because of this supply strain, Constellation Energy has not significantly increased its capacity over the past 8 quarters.

Looking forward, while AI data centers rely on an immense requirement for power, nuclear power provides a minority of this supply. In 2024, natural gas supplied over 40% of the electricity for data centers. Nuclear power, on the other hand, provided only 20%. Given the supply strains for uranium, this disparity will likely persist. Furthermore, CEG’s valuation is currently high with a forward P/E of 33, 70% higher than its sector median. Therefore, even if CEG does see a strong improvement in growth, its valuation leaves little room for a higher share price.

Energy Transfer LP: Lacking Fundamental Growth

The second factor is Energy Transfer, TPZ’s #2 holding, at almost 7% weight. While the energy transportation company may seem bright on the surface, there are issues underneath. Energy Transfer, unlike Constellation Energy, focuses on the transportation of natural gas and makes its profits through fees to transport energy. However, the company has not been able to manage its revenues to drive growth. This problem is exacerbated by a net income margin of just 5.7% along with much of its profits going towards its 7.7% dividend yield.

This lack of profitability and limited growth was further witnessed by the company’s cancellation of the Lake Charles liquified natural gas export facility in Louisiana. Quantifiably, this is also measured in the company’s YoY revenue growth of -4.7%. Overall, ET’s fundamental metrics do not drive a strong holding for TPZ overall. Due to its management of cash flow, Energy Transfer has not shown an ability to see significant growth, despite the strong demand for natural gas.

Vistra Corporation: Concerning Risk Factors Beneath the Surface

The third consideration is Vistra Corporation at about 5.5% weight. While not a significant weight for TPZ, VST is another holding that is representative of the fund’s selection methodology. The Texas-based company provides retail electricity and power generation within the United States and would therefore seem to be a strong income provider. Indeed, in its recent quarterly presentation, Vistra boats strong EBITDA growth and cash generation. However, upon closer examination, we see $18.5B in net debt which has eroded its 30% EBITDA margin down to a 6.7% net income margin. Additionally, its 2.6 ratio of net debt to adjusted EBITDA drives a reliance on pricing power within the electricity market and access to capital.

Looking forward, VST also has an undesirable valuation. With a forward P/E of 26, the company has a much higher valuation than many peer energy producers, adding another concerning factor. The company has also experienced almost a 20% increase in capital expenditures between 2024 and 2025. While this is currently covered by strong cash flow, growing CAPEX increases risk for the company if not sufficiently supported in coming years.

Current Valuation and 2026 Outlook

TPZ’s top three holdings have all experienced negative returns over the past year. This decline has resulted in a flat recent share price return for TPZ as a whole and significant underperformance for the fund compared to peer energy ETFs. Alternatively, VOLT and GRID have both surpassed the S&P 500 Index over the past six months, presenting significant opportunity cost for investment in TPZ.

Despite recent share price underperformance, TPZ’s valuation is not more attractive than peers when comparing its P/E and P/B ratios. Looking forward, TPZ’s valuation and the concerning factors for its top holdings, as previously discussed, signal continued underperformance for the remainder of 2026.

Valuation Metrics for TPZ and Compared Energy Infrastructure Competitors

TPZ

GRID

VOLT

IGF

P/E ratio

20.65

26.72

28.39

20.91

P/B ratio

3.50

3.64

2.42

2.41

Source: Compiled by Author from Multiple Sources, 17 Jan 26

Risks to Investors

Due to its energy concentration, investors in TPZ will experience unique cyclical and policy-specific risks. These factors induce volatility in the price of energy such as natural gas which is used to produce electricity. While TPZ’s performance is not directly tied to price volatility, uncertain prices induce uncertainty for production that is a core part of many TPZ holdings. Just a few years ago, natural gas price volatility was over 90% in certain quarters. Therefore, while residential, commercial, and industrial energy demand will remain persistently high, price volatility will remain a risk factor.

The volatility discussed above, along with the alarming signals for TPZ’s top holdings present risk for investors. Given the muted performance that will likely persist, I see the fund as a sell even for investors seeking income through dividend yields.

Concluding Summary

TPZ is a high-fee fund with limited upside looking forward. While its energy holdings produce a strong dividend yield, investors should not fixate on this one quality. Instead, when examined holistically, TPZ will likely underperform due to a combination of growth, profitability, and valuation issues.

data.title: MSTY Through A Bear Regime: What The First Full Cycle Reveals
data.description:
I maintain a Sell rating on the Yieldmax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (MSTY). I have been warning about structural issues in the approach and its implications since March last year, at a time when interest in this high-yield product was picking up. Since August peaks, the interest has been waning a bit, and recent AUM activity suggests investor exhaustion since October.

Other than MSTR's fall since October, nothing has actually changed in the ETF's structure or methodology. This analysis actually dives deeper into MSTY's performance in a first regime since launch when MSTR has really collapsed and finds the performance in line with expectations in such a regime. Investors who flocked to MSTY in mid 2025 were always riding MSTR risks, and if they were indeed bullish on MSTR, then MSTY was not the best tool to monetize it. In fact, MSTY has done relatively well (as is expected) during this fall in MSTR from October 2025.

Since my last coverage, another important development in MSTY is the reverse split that has potentially sent negative signals, along with a crashing share price (in an unsupportive environment). NAV erosion and high yields go hand in hand, and that is a concern, but I am more worried about total return underperformance - that has importantly remained where it was since inception. Unlike in the case of ULTY, where positive changes have emerged in terms of the underlying portfolio selection, option strategy changes, and even some yield moderation, no material changes were visible in MSTY when I checked its holdings and trades. MSTY does not have scope for portfolio level changes like ULTY (given it rides a single stock). But yields and option strategies (like the introduction of net put positions like in ULTY) could have changed - and they have not, as per what a look at the portfolio reveals.

Waning Appetite

The waning appetite phenomenon I talked about at the start is clearly visible in the AUM versus price charts. AUM changes are broadly attributable to inflow, reinvestment of payouts, and price changes, so a clean attribution of waning AUM to either variable is not perfect. But the rise in interest from April 2025 to August is still visible. This is helped by a direct impact of share price stability and its indirect impact on investor interest (high yields on a stable base). The same observations are true for the decline since August, accelerated since October (coincidentally, since my last coverage).

The drop in interest since October follows a similar trail as the price graph. So, that is certainly a factor, mechanically dragging the AUM. However, we can confidently assert that inflows have certainly slowed compared to the earlier regimes, where AUM was growing despite a flat price trajectory.

Yields, NAV Erosion and Reverse Split

The reverse split has grabbed most of the attention in the past few months. Standalone, that is not a negative development in my view. A yield product trading at $100, say, drops to $1 due to heavy yields, may resort to reverse splits for operational reasons or optics. Even an income plan that actually works may do the same. The reverse split per se is not an acknowledgment of issues in a strategy. The core issue is therefore not the yield (and NAV erosion) as well. The core issue is whether reinvestments would have mattered - that is, total returns. If the drop to $1 was accompanied by, say, a payout of $200, reinvesting into a systematically growing total return machine would have been a no-brainer - agnostic of how many units I am forced to hold. I will evaluate the total return performance in a subsequent section.

NAV erosion is an issue for investors not reinvesting payouts. This is because even in a systematically growing strategy, there is less left on the table on the way down (you take the payouts while the underlying is going down) and do not gain as much because of lower stakes on the rebound. As an example, say you invested $100 in MSTR. It travels down to $50. You have taken out $20 systematically as payouts during this fall, reducing the stakes further from $50 to $30. When MSTR rebounds back to $100, i.e., doubles, your investments also double, but only to $60. Adding the $20 already harvested, the net position reads $80, not $100. MSTY, of course, adds an option layer that could plug this $20 hole in the net outcome, but as we will see in the Performance section later (and have seen in my past analyses), the option layer's upside capping mechanism and limited drawdown benefits do not work out very well over full cycles.

One way to address this issue of NAV erosion is to balance yields - particularly if investors intend to use MSTY as a long-term income plan rather than a tactical ploy in flat to pressured MSTR regimes. There, MSTY has not really made any major changes yet to moderate the yield levels. In fact, MSTY's yield levels have only become more aggressive since October.

Portfolio Deep Dive

I downloaded the latest intraday trades and current holdings reported on the MSTY website to understand the strategy's current positioning. The core long position is implemented using options (synthetic position) with positions across February 2026 and March 2026. These are rolled over near or before expiry to maintain the net long effect on MSTR.

Covered calls are written for the near month expiry, as expected, but spread across strikes ranging from $175 to $192.5. The call writing ladder formation ensures some upside capture compared to a simpler call writing at-the-money structure. While some of these strikes can be deemed at-the-money, significant call writing far out of the money shows upside capture is not as restricted as in theory. Another factor that supports upside capture is that the covered call layer accounts for ~70% of the net long position (February and March combined). This balances income positioning with upside retention.

There is a call spread structure also seen, with long positions for the near month expiry for strikes further out - between $182.5 and $200. This layer ensures unlimited upside is captured beyond a point when MSTR rallies, which is great for reducing the upside cap drag. However, I could not see a balanced long exposure here. The call layer is marginally smaller than the short call layer (check the number of share equivalents in the figure above for context) - an attempt to optimize income while retaining upside capture potential. Since the methodology is clearly active, this layer's size could change depending on how management views MSTR in the near month. Since MSTR is down and consolidating now, we could be seeing a bigger upside capture layer now. We may see less long call convexity in another regime.

While the holdings reveal more upside capture possibilities than a theoretical at-the-money covered call strategy (through partial option coverage, and a call spread structure), that also comes with lower income potential or drawdown mitigation possibilities. The funding required for buying the long call layer (in pursuit of upside capture) erodes the option premiums gained from call writing. The balance is so demanding (between income and upside capture), that the ask becomes very high of the active management - and getting the balance right in every market condition is indeed a tougher ask. This was the case in October when I last wrote - and structurally I do not see much difference today in the portfolio. There is no additional long put layer to cushion falls (for example) or material structural changes to address the inherent issues with such a methodology.

This active management requirement shows up in the intraday log I downloaded for 16th January 2026. Assuming it is indicative of how the ETF typically operates, I saw around a 2-3% increase in core exposure in just one day. This could be adjusting to inflow changes, rolling over positions, or speculative positioning—but it shows the amount of activity required even in the core position daily.

On the covered call credit spread layer, I saw over 180k contracts traded. This could also be due to various reasons like a change in outlook or new coverage for added net core exposure or even rollovers. The amount of activity and active calls involved looks very high to me - a tall ask while MSTY tries to manufacture yields every day. This is not necessarily a negative, but introduces more risk of errors in active calls and certainly inflates expenses (close to 1%). Importantly, despite all the activity, it does not create a compelling performance graph over the long term.

Expected Performance Trajectory

As I mentioned before in this analysis (and across older articles), total returns remain my biggest concern when it comes to MSTY. For investments made at inception, MSTY investors are still behind MSTR's total returns - this is now confirmed after MSTR's drawdown had shown more cyclical data across regimes.

The second aspect I wanted to show, now that we have more drawdown data that was missing before, is how MSTY's option layer does not generate meaningful alpha over the long term - something I have been talking about theoretically so far. In a regime spanning from early 2024 to today (just less than 2 years), MSTR has not gone anywhere on an end-to-end basis. MSTY does not meaningfully add any expected alpha from the option layer, nor does it reduce the volatility of the trajectory.

It is actually great that MSTY has not eroded systematically. It has actually captured upsides better (as seen from the portfolio setup analysis) than theory would have suggested. However, that has come at the cost of systematic income accumulation from the option layer over time. MSTY at best becomes a proxy for MSTR, following it closely. But introducing huge active management risks and expensive turnover and forcing a yield, investors in MSTR (aiming for systematic income from the underlying directly) can choose to set.

The AUM pressures have ironically come in a period when MSTY has actually delivered on its promise of reducing drawdowns - it is down by ~32% when MSTR has crashed by ~39%. This is expected behavior, and waning investor behavior in a relatively good period for MSTY shows most investors were actually betting on MSTR rather than MSTY's relative advantages.

Retaining the Sell

My recommendation for MSTY investors remains the same. MSTR-agnostic investors should stay away. Those who believe in MSTR's rebound or long-term strength should invest in MSTR and withdraw income (because that is what MSTY ends up doing anyway). Only for tactical investors, MSTY could make sense - it will cushion drawdowns, although in a limited capacity, and generate some income in flat markets. Looking at the AUM activity, MSTY was certainly not being looked at as a tactical tool - so for a vast majority of MSTY investors, it is a sell.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson: Tempering Expectations After A Massive 2025 (Downgrade)
data.description:
Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) has a full head of steam coming into the Q4 earnings report later this week. Shares are up by more than 50% in the past 12 months, making it among the leading DJIA stocks in the last year. But I'm downgrading shares, purely on valuation.

The chart is magnificent, while earnings growth is steady in the years ahead. Unfortunately, the low-20s P/E is a bit too rich today. The high-quality Health Care name is by no means a sell/short idea, though. With upside growth catalysts on tap and a solid 2.4% dividend yield, the management team continues to deliver. I expect a bottom-line beat on the 21st.

Shares are up 24% just since my Q3 2025 analysis, outperforming the S&P 500 by almost 20 percentage points on a total return basis.

JNJ Trounces XLV and SPY Returns YoY

Back in October, JNJ reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q3 non-GAAP EPS of $2.80 topped the Wall Street consensus target of $2.76, while revenue of $24 billion, up 7% from the same period a year earlier, was a material $240 million beat. Moreover, the company raised its FY 2025 sales forecast.

The double-beat and raise was met with muted applause from investors. Shares were precisely unchanged in the session that followed. Looking forward to the January 21 Q4 report, the options market prices in a modest 2.7% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the release. Shares of the $529 billion Health Care large-cap company feature a low 21% implied volatility reading, and short interest on the stock is modest at 0.65%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, JNJ tallied $24 billion in net sales for just the second time in its storied history. Its Innovated Medicine segment grew revenue to $15.6 over the July through September stretch, driven by double-digit gains in 11 of its brands, including DARZALEX, ERLEADA, CARVYKTI, TREMFYA, and SPRAVATO.

Q3 Numbers

Elsewhere, JNJ's MedTech sales hit $8.4 billion, fueled by strong growth in Cardiovascular, particularly Electrophysiology and Abiomed, alongside contributions from Surgery and Vision.

Particularly impressive was the firm's 69.6% gross margin, along with healthy R&D spending that appears to be paying off. From a shareholder perspective, JNJ intends to separate its Orthopaedics business by the end of next year... such a spinoff could create value (as has been the case marketwide this cycle). Recently, there were no immediate volatility catalysts noted at last week's JPMorgan Health Care Conference.

Finally, the management team increased FY 2025 revenue guidance to be in the range of $93.0-$93.4 billion and maintained adjusted operational EPS at $10.63-$10.73.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be about steady in FY 2026 and through the out year. The company could reach $12 in operating per-share earnings on a forward basis by the middle of 2026. But sales growth is only expected to be in the mid-single digits.

Hence, there have been a high 15 sell-side downgrades in the past 12 months, compared to just a pair of upgrades. Still, profitability trends are sublime, with free cash flow per share of $7.76 (about a 3% FCF yield).

JNJ: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, we run into issues. JNJ now trades at more than 21 times expected EPS. That's a near-market multiple and more than a full turn pricier compared to its long-term average. I simply think that's stretched.

If we assume a generous 20x multiple and $11.50 of current-year non-GAAP EPS, then shares should trade near $230. That just doesn't leave much of a margin of safety anymore, particularly considering the 5.6x forward price-to-sales ratio-25% above the long-term average.

JNJ: Above Its LTA P/E

Key risks for JNJ include slower growth in its MedTech area as industry competition rises, lighter revenue gains in new products, and litigation risks that are seemingly always present with the company. On the Q3 earnings call, there were some doubts over JNJ's ability to expand to new markets, though the management team was quick to point out that its pipeline is solid.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Wednesday, January 21 BMO. Shares trade ex a $1.30 dividend on Tuesday, February 24.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Year Ahead

The Technical Take

With shares close to intrinsic value, JNJ's technical situation is very strong. Notice in the chart below that a powerful uptrend remains intact, dating back to last June. The long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend, while the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph has ranged in a bullish zone between 45 and 80. Simply put, the bears have had little chance to pounce on the premium valuation.

Moreover, shares went above and beyond the measured move upside price target of near $210 (blue in the below chart). For now, the rising short-term 50 DMA acts as support, and it has confluence with an uptrend support line. So, $205 (as of this writing) should be your buy point-for those seeking a 'buy the dip' play leading into and after the Q4 earnings report.

JNJ: Support at the 50dma, Bullish RSI Trend, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on JNJ. This DJIA leader and Health Care sector stalwart has returned 50% just since Q2 2025. Shares now appear close to fair value, but the chart is incredibly bullish.

data.title: Small Caps Are Leading The Way
data.description:
I have written articles on this blog over the past year or year and a half about small company stocks beginning to receive investor attention as they began to outperform large company stocks. The small-cap outperformance tended to be short-lived, though, as investors turned their attention back to the so-called Magnificent 7 or 8 mega-cap technology stocks and, more broadly, large company stocks. Of late, though, small-cap stocks are once again leading many other asset classes. As the below table shows and sorted on the 3-month total return column, the leading asset classes are micro-cap, small-cap and mid-cap stocks. The iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR) is up 9.68% versus the S&P 500 Index, up 5.0% over the past three months.

Another small company stock index is the Russell 2000 Index, which is investable by using the Russell 2000 ETF (IWM). This ETF is now outperforming the large-cap S&P 500 ETF (SPY) on a 1-year basis, 19.52% versus 18.14%, respectively. A difference between the S&P 600 Index and the Russell 2000 Index, both focusing on small companies, is the S&P 600 Index requires companies comprising the index to be profitable. However, this profitability requirement is not a metric for the Russell 2000 Index, and a little more than 40% of the companies in this index are unprofitable.

A question investors ask is whether this outperforming trend for small caps is sustainable, and there are some underlying factors that suggest maybe it is. Because a large percentage of small-cap companies are unprofitable, they tend to carry more debt financing on their balance sheet to operate the business. With the Fed lowering interest rates and expected to continue to do so this year, debt financing cost becomes less burdensome for companies using debt. Additionally, as seen in the below table from LSEG I/B/E/S, earnings growth for small companies is expected to be significantly positive through the third quarter of 2027.

Certainly, this outperformance with small-cap stocks might be fleeting; however, some underlying factors like lower interest rates and strong earnings growth seem to suggest otherwise. Up until recently, momentum has favored the Mag 7 or 8 stocks, but as seen in the below chart, all but two are underperforming the S&P 500 Index over the last three months. In short, this rotation into other asset classes would make the equity market rally sustainable through 2026, of course not higher in a straight line.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: WaFd Looks Cheap For A Reason, And That's Why I'm Interested
data.description:
Thesis

This is my first time covering WaFd, Inc. (WAFD), a Seattle-based holding company for WaFd Bank, with more than 200 offices across nine Western states.

On momentum, WAFD’s about even with the S&P 500 (SP500) over the past six months, but meaningfully behind on one year and three year total returns.

Nonetheless, after analyzing the latest earnings, I’m bullish on WAFD because it’s cheap, margins look close to bottoming, core deposits are improving, and past cleanup cycles suggest earnings can recover once credit stress stabilizes.

WaFd Q1 2026 Results Overview

  • WaFd, Inc. reported net income ... of $60.5 million or $0.79 per diluted share.
  • Net interest income increased $1.2 million from the prior quarter.
  • Total noninterest income increased $1.9 million compared to the prior quarter at $20.3 million.
  • Losses of $408,000 taken on certain equity method investments in the quarter compared to gains on those investments of $815,000 in the prior quarter.

  • The net interest margin was 2.7% in the December quarter compared to 2.71% for the September quarter.

  • For the spot rate as of December 2025 period end, the yield on interest-earning assets was 5.05%, while the cost of interest-bearing liabilities was 2.76% with a resulting margin of 2.77%.

Margins got squeezed this quarter for a pretty familiar reason in banking. When loans are placed on nonaccrual status, the bank is no longer allowed to record interest income the normal way. Even though the bank uses hedging strategies to reduce the impact, those hedges only partially offset the loss and don't eliminate it. Repricing still hurts. We’ve seen this movie before. In earlier cleanup cycles, banks like this ate the loss, kept quiet about it, and got back to running the business.

  • Total investments and mortgage-backed securities increased $728 million during the quarter, funded primarily by the increase in borrowings of $671 million.
  • Investment purchases were primarily discount-priced agency mortgage-backed securities with an effective yield of 4.93%.
  • The 3 remaining basis points relates to our purchase of mortgage-backed securities during the quarter, as I mentioned, with a net yield of 4.93%.

On top of that, they’re leaning more into agency MBS to replace runoff in the old loan book. This is essentially giving up yield in order to maintain liquidity. It isn’t really a voluntary decision, it’s a limitation the bank has to operate within. They still need to protect earnings, but they can’t stretch for risk, so this is where they land. That said, the setup looks a bit better from here. Funding costs seem like they’re settling out, especially if the Fed stays on hold for most of the year. Cuts don’t look likely until midyear anyway, given how firm the data still is.

  • Loan originations and advances for the quarter outpaced repayments and payoffs in our active loan types with originations at $1.1 billion and repayments and payoffs at $1 billion.
  • This loan production was centered in commercial and industrial of 46%, commercial real estate of 23% and construction of 25%.

On the loan side, some of the active portfolios like C&I (Commercial & Industrial), CRE (Commercial Real Estate), and construction are still growing. New loans are being added at a faster pace than existing loans are being paid back. That part isn’t shocking. Businesses don’t stop needing credit just because rates are higher. If anything, higher rates and tariff pressures seem to be changing why they borrow. More working capital.

  • Adversely classified loans decreased by $51 million in the quarter and now represent 2.94% of net loans compared to 3.16% as of the September quarter and 1.97% as of December 2024.
  • Total criticized loans increased by $30 million to 4.6% of net loans compared to ...4.39% as of the September quarter and 2.54% as of December of 2024.
  • Nonperforming assets increased to $203 million or 0.75% of total assets from $143 million or 0.54% at September 30, 2025.
  • The change is due to nonaccrual loans increasing by $62.7 million or 49% since September 30, 2025.
  • Delinquent loans increased to 1.07% of total loans at December 31, 2025, compared to 0.6% at September 30, 2025, and 0.3% at December 31, 2024.
  • Outstanding balances to these relationships amount to $58 million collectively.

But I don’t love what’s sitting underneath it. Increasingly criticized and nonperforming assets, especially in manufacturing and energy, indicate borrowers squeezed by higher borrowing costs and trade policy supply chain disruption. Tariffs are making the situation worse by increasing material costs. As a result, some firms are delaying their investments, and this is putting additional pressure on loan performance. Commercial real estate is still a hot area to watch. Yet, offices are clearly lagging, even though the broader market looks like it’s stabilizing. The main issue, of course, is hybrid work, which keeps demand soft. On top of that, banks are getting more selective. They’re tightening standards where they need to, mostly to avoid piling into the same losses.

  • Total deposits decreased by $21 million during the quarter.
  • Noninterest-bearing deposits increasing $125 million or 4.9%.
  • Interest-bearing deposits increasing $434 million or 4.5%, while time deposits decreased $580 million or 6.4%.
  • Core deposits ended the quarter at 79.7% of total deposits, up slightly from the September quarter at 77.9%.
  • Noninterest-bearing deposits ended the quarter at 12.6% of total deposits.
  • The loan-to-deposit ratio ended the quarter at 92.7%.

Deposits show a more positive picture. Growth in noninterest-bearing accounts indicates that efforts to build business relationships are working, and this growth is helping to offset money leaving time deposit accounts. The increase in core funding strengthens liquidity, which lowers the need to rely on wholesale borrowing. This is especially important in an environment where peer institutions are experiencing only modest loan growth despite overall economic stability. All the same, I’d still be careful. Smaller banks carry more risk because they’re more exposed to commercial real estate, and they don’t have the same capital cushion the big banks do.

  • Total noninterest expense decreased $1.3 million or 1.2% from the prior quarter.
  • Decreased expenses combined with increased income resulted in a decrease in our efficiency ratio for the current quarter to 55.3% compared to 56.8% in the prior quarter.

Meanwhile, expenses edged lower, largely because compensation and technology outlays, for now at least, didn’t run wild. As a result, efficiency ratios drifted back toward the levels management says it’s been aiming for all along. Still, they’re putting money into things like wealth management and SBA lending, which means there’s a real trade-off as they try to diversify revenue. Noninterest income also got a lift from selling properties. That helped this quarter. Equity investments made the results less steady, and if the markets become more unstable, that volatility could easily happen again.

Looking ahead, the idea is to make more commercial and industrial loans and pull in more transaction deposits, until those deposits account for about 20% of the overall mix. According to management, reorganized teams will be used to support this effort, with the goal of growing the active portfolio by 8–12% over the next few years.

WaFd's Valuation

So, right now, WaFd trades at about 11.7x earnings, at least based on FAST Graphs. That gives a decent snapshot of how the market’s valuing it today.

This number combines its historical average P/E of 14.17 with forward estimates that assume 10.69% earnings growth as the bank shifts more toward C&I and CRE lending.

At this stock price, WaFd appears undervalued compared to an estimated fair value of $41.51. If the move into C&I and CRE loans delivers the expected 8%–12% growth, the stock could have about 28% upside from current levels. The forecasts assume a better deposit mix, with noninterest-bearing money up to 20%, but only if credit holds together while nonaccruals climb to 0.75% of assets. The tariff shock landing on manufacturing borrowers injects another layer of volatility into the system. And, as history suggests, commercial real estate risks didn’t just accompany downturns but actively magnified them. Also, pressure on profit margins from MBS yields at 4.93% could reduce the expected 30.97% annual return if interest rate cuts don't happen.

Finally, energy exposure in C&I loans could turn into charge-offs if oil keeps sliding. At the same time, the Street is still modeling about 11.5% EPS growth this year, so you don't have to be in a rush to get in.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I like WaFd here. It looks cheap, and if the commercial pivot actually kicks in and loan growth follows, I see close to 30% upside. Because of that, I’m comfortable starting with a small position this week. If you’re a value investor and you can live with bank exposure, I think it makes sense to build a position over time. But if you’re sensitive to CRE or energy risk, though, I’d stay away.

data.title: CGBL: A Solid ETF For Modest Risk-Tolerance Investors To Earn Healthy Returns
data.description:
Capital Group Core Balanced ETF’s (CGBL) potential to capitalize on the bullish market conditions while limiting a risk factor makes it an attractive investment vehicle for the modest risk-tolerance investors. CGBL’s total return of 15% in 2025 was in-line with the S&P 500 index. The fund uses an active portfolio management strategy of holding stakes in growth stocks with the potential to pay dividends. CGBL also dedicates a notable portion of its portfolio to the fixed asset classes to limit a risk factor. The fund’s expense ratio is low and dividend yield is greater than the market average. Therefore, I initiate coverage of CGBL with a buy rating.

CGBL’s Performance Review and 2026 Price Target

Launched in the late 2023, Capital Group Core Balanced ETF continues to enhance investor confidence through its strong performances in the bullish market conditions. CGBL’s average annual share price growth of 19% since its inception seems exceptional, give a modest risk factor. In addition, it offers a solid dividend yield of 1.9% with the potential to increase dividends at a double digit rate. In the trailing twelve months, the fund lifted its dividend by 16%.

I expect CGBL to continue its average annual price growth momentum in 2026. Therefore, I anticipate CGBL to end fiscal 2026 around $43 per share. I attribute my price outlook to two key factors: CGBL’s portfolio management strategy and bullish broader market conditions.

Currently, Wall Street expects the S&P 500 to hit 8000 points mark by the end of 2026, reflecting 15% upside from the current level. However, I believe the market can exceed analysts price target, given the robust earnings growth forecast for 2026.

FactSet data indicates that the S&P 500 is likely to experience 15% earnings growth in 2026, the highest growth rate since 2021. In addition, the economic growth and interest rate trends also appear favorable for the stock market. A risk of recession continues to mitigate while interest rate declined significantly from the recent peak.

CGBL’s Impressive Portfolio Strategy Can Achieve Healthy Returns

Capital Group Core Balanced ETF seeks to deliver healthy-risk adjusted returns in the short to long run. Unlike traditional index-focused fund’s, CGBL uses a multi-asset approach in a smart way.

Currently, the fund dedicates almost one third of its portfolio to defensive assets while the rest of portfolio comprises growth stocks with the potential to pay dividends. As it is an actively managed ETF, its management can make significant changes its portfolio to align with the market trends.

CGBL’s top two holdings, making up 35% of its portfolio weight, are defensive assets. Capital Group Core Plus Income ETF (CGCP) is its largest holding, which seeks to invest in bonds and other debt securities . Its dividend yield is above 5%. Whereas, Capital Group Core Bond ETF (CGCB) is its second largest holding with a portfolio weight of 14%. Both ETFs are managed by the Capital Group and its partners.

Besides the defensive stakes, the rest of its portfolio is composed of well-established and fast-growing equities. Broadcom (AVGO), Microsoft (MSFT), Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) and NVIDIA (NVDA) are its largest holdings. All these tech companies are among the key players in AI market.

For example, Broadcom, Taiwan Semiconductor and NVIDIA are the leading players in the AI chip market. Broadcom’s share price rallied 57% in the last twelve months while Taiwan Semiconductor and NVIDIA experienced a price surge of 40% and 65%. Their robust earnings growth potential also enable them to distribute healthy dividends to shareholders. On the other hand, Microsoft and Alphabet are leading in the AI software and communications market.

CGBL’s portfolio is also diversified towards the financials, industrials, consumer cyclical and consumer defensive stocks. ATI Inc (ATI), Philip Morris (PM) and Booking Holdings (BKNG) are among its top holdings. These fast-growing companies have the potential to generate a solid share price and dividend growth. ATI is an aerospace & defense company. Its share price soared 124% in the last twelve months.

CGBL’s portfolio also includes many other well-established and fast growing companies, such as Meta (META), Apple (AAPL), Vertex Pharma (VRTX), Boeing (BA), Mastercard (MA), General Electric (GE) and Gilead Sciences (GILD).

Overall, it appears that the fund’s portfolio strategy can limit a risk factor, offer an above average dividend yield and deliver a solid price appreciation. I believe this strategy can enable modest risk-tolerance investors to earn healthy returns in the bullish market conditions.

In the case of bearish trend, the management can adjust its equity holdings. Its stake in the fixed income ETFs can also mitigate the impact of stock market downtrend. Its portfolio is managed by a team of experienced managers with most of them having nearly 30 years of experience in the investment industry.

Other Key Characteristics

CGBL trades around $36 per share. Its low per share price can help investors buy shares in a large quantity. In addition, the fund also appears fairly priced based on its valuation. CGBL’s price to book and price earnings ratios are in-line with the market average.

CGBL’s fast-growing liquidity reflects increasing investors confidence. In just over two years since launch, CGBL’s average shares trading volume reached 900K level compared to the median of all ETFs of 37K. The fund’s assets under management also exceeded $4.5 billion mark. In addition, given its expense ratio of 0.33%, the fund offers a cheap way to access a multi-asset portfolio. An expense ratio of the median of all ETFs is 0.50%.

Risk Factors to Consider

According to the Seeking Alpha data, CGBL’s standard deviation of 6.90 and annualized volatility of 12% is less than the median of all ETFs of 9.69 and 16.60%, respectively. Besides the technical factors, the fund can respond to tech-related downtrends, given its healthy stake in the technology, consumer cyclical and communication stocks. The broader stock market trends can also negatively impact its performance. Overall, the fund carries a modest risk factor.

Final Thoughts

In the tech-driven bullish market trends, the modest risk-tolerance investors can also earn lofty returns by chasing investment vehicles, such as Capital Group Core Balanced ETF. CGBL smartly uses a multi-asset approach to limit a risk factor while capitalizing on the bullish market trends. The fund seeks to dedicate 50% to 75% of its portfolio to the high momentum stocks with the potential to pay dividends. The rest of its portfolio comprises fixed income assets. Its expense ratio is lower than median of all ETFs, liquidity is strong and dividend yield is above average.

data.title: Cargojet: Strong Buy Despite Earnings Cuts And A 32% Rally
data.description:
Cargojet (CGJTF) (CJT:CA), an enabling airline force for express delivery in Canada, saw its share prices increase 32.2% since my last report, outperforming the S&P 500’s 2.3% decline. Given the substantial increase in the stock price, it is a good time to review whether there are any substantial changes in earnings expectations for the fourth quarter and beyond and update my price target accordingly.

Amounts in this report are in Canadian dollars unless mentioned otherwise.

Analysts Have Turned Less Optimistic On Q4 2025 Earnings

For the fourth quarter, analysts are expecting Cargojet to report revenues between $230.3 million and $265 million with a consensus estimate of $251 million, marking a 14.4% year-on-year decline. We note that the consensus estimate dropped 14.3% over the past six months and 12.3% over the past three months, with all analysts adjusting estimates downward.

Analysts are expecting $0.99 in earnings per share with a range between $0.71 and $1.39, marking a 42.3% decline. Estimates were adjusted downward 47.8% over the past six months and 50.9% over the past three months. Just like with the estimate for revenues, all analysts revised estimates downward.

Why Did Estimates Decline For Cargojet?

Generally, it is not a good sign when we see revisions to estimates as big as we are seeing for Cargojet with no upward revisions. At the same time, we note that the company is dealing with some indirect impact from the trade war.

Cargojet is positioned as a company that enables express delivery from China. So, the company has a strong moat in the domestic market. We also note that most of the goods that cross the borders of the US, Canada, and Mexico do so by rail and road, meaning that Cargojet should be less affected by trade tariffs. However, there are some indirect impacts on Cargojet.

One of the impacts is that in the ACMI operations, meaning Aircraft, Crew, Maintenance, and Insurance contracts, DHL has opted to tweak the network. Instead of flying transatlantic, the company shifted Cargojet’s capacity more towards shorter-distance routes to South America, which reduces the total block hours executed. In the third quarter, the company also saw direct flights between China and Canada reduce, but with a survival expected in the fourth quarter, while the elimination of the de minimis exemption also reduced volumes of routes through Canada. The company is trying to offset some of the impacts by adding new destinations such as Liege to the network, but the bottom line is that total block hours have reduced and revenues have declined.

So, while domestic network revenues held up well, shifts in the long-haul operations caused lower block hours execution and overall declines in ACMI and charter revenues. That is a pressure that does impact the business quite significantly, as Cargojet has a cost structure that is optimized for higher revenues without having the ability to cut much in the costs. Furthermore, the company also noted that while Q4 is expected to be better than Q3 driven by the holiday volumes, the increase would be less than the company normally sees on a sequential basis. That was the final push needed for analysts to revise estimates.

Cargojet Stock Still Trades At A Major Discount

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Dollar amounts below are in USD.

For Cargojet, we note that there has been no material change to the estimates compared to my prior analysis. The reason is that the aforementioned reductions in analyst estimates were already part of my previously revised estimates. From there, the estimates did not substantially change apart from an upward revision on free cash flow driven by the way free cash flow is calculated for Cargojet. We see that the stock is substantially undervalued against peers and against its own trading multiple. The peer group valuation would justify a stock price of $96.55, pulling in no forward earnings into the target window. This would imply 47% upside. However, we do note that Cargojet is rather well optimized for higher block hours than are currently being executed, and the company does not seem to have many levers to bring costs down without downsizing the company as a whole. So, the upside we see may not be fully unlocked. I have valued the stock at a 7.5x EV/EBITDA, which may be more appropriate for the time being, and that brings us to a price target of $79.37, implying a 21% upside.

The challenge that Cargojet is currently facing is that there is little growth in sales as strength in the domestic market is at least partially offset by the pressures faced in the ACMI business without major cost adjustments possible without altering the scale of the company. In terms of capital expenditures, we see a bump this year, and that is likely a capital expenditure figure that the company would not have had. It does seem counterintuitive that the company would increase capital expenditures at a time when block hours and thus utilization are down. However, we do note that CapEx is split between growth CapEx, which has been reducing, and maintenance CapEx, acquiring airplanes to replace existing airplanes. Furthermore, we note that fleet investment decisions are not made for the next quarter and not for the next year but are part of a multi-year strategy in which Cargojet does need to size the fleet according to the customer’s future needs rather than today’s depressed needs. So, the CapEx we do see covers, to some extent, future growth requirements and investments for fleet quality to drive margins in contracts.

By design, Cargojet runs the business on lower cash levels, and that is driven by some predictability in the business as the company is shielded on contracts through minimum guarantees. We also see that back in the cash performance. Even with lower utilization and higher CapEx, the company is not expected to burn cash, and that allows the company to return value to shareholders as well even when the market is not in great shape. We even note that for 2025, I have modelled in share repurchases well below what the company could handle. We also see that the company is well within the 2x-3x net debt leverage range that I consider healthy.

Conclusion: Cargojet Remains Attractive

While the stock price has increased substantially and analyst estimates have come down over the past months, from a valuation perspective, I believe that the stock remains attractive. We see that the company should be able to absorb a higher CapEx figure this year while still driving a positive free cash flow in a depressed market. Furthermore, net debt leverage even after share repurchases looks healthy, and I believe that speaks to the quality of the business.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NXG: Aligned To Benefit From Data Center Demand Growth (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

Infrastructure is always an interesting area of the market because it consists of companies that improve the quality of our lives. This essential nature of the sector has always made infrastructure a solid place to park long-term capital. NXG NextGen Infrastructure Income Fund (NXG) takes a bit of a modernized approach by including a larger focus on digital infrastructure. When I previously covered NXG, I issued a hold rating due to the inconsistent dividend coverage. Now that we've crossed into the new year, I wanted to reassess the fund's value proposition.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that NXG's share price has increased by slightly over 1%. When including all distributions that were paid out to investors, the total return jumps to 15.5% over the same time frame. NXG now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 12.8% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. While the dividend income can be a great motivator for a specific group of investors, I believe that the risks must be measured. I think that NXG and its positions are aligned for growth potential as demand for data centers grows. NXG's emphasis on income generation may limit total returns compared to traditional ETFs.

The fund now trades at a slight premium to NAV valuation. Despite this, I believe that NXG has attractive growth potential going forward, which can be driven by the growing demand around AI and data centers. After assessing some of the fund's top holdings, I was able to locate some concrete instances where we can already see the shift towards data center growth taking place. However, I do have some caution about the fund's performance compared to more traditional ETFs. The focus on income generation can ultimately limit returns over a longer holding period. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying portfolio strategy and how the fund is positioned for growth.

Strategy Captures Data Center Growth

According to the fund's latest fact sheet, NXG has total net assets of $285.7M that are allocated across a blend of equities and debt securities. NXG's primary focus is to generate a high current income and attractive total return from infrastructure investments. However, NXG takes a more specific approach by focusing on the infrastructure of tomorrow, which leans more heavily towards technological infrastructure. So while traditional infrastructure investments invest in things like roads, ports, airports, or natural gas distribution, NXG focuses on the modern era of infrastructure. Just to name a few examples, this includes things such as:

  • Water
  • Electric Vehicles
  • Data Storage
  • Wireless
  • Cloud Data
  • Renewable Energy

As a result, the fund allocates its assets across the many sub-sectors of infrastructure. NXG is most heavily weighted towards utilities at 24.2% of assets. However, I wanted to specifically look at the top holdings of the fund because I believe they directly position NXG to participate in the growing demand of data centers and positive momentum of AI. Looking at the top positions, we can see that Vistra Corp. (VST) is the largest holding at 5.67% of assets. This is followed by many other notable companies, such as Talen Energy (TLN), Constellation Energy (CEG), NRG Energy (NRG), and Targa Resources (TRGP), just to name a few.

While the rest of the market focuses on the large-cap companies like NVIDIA (NVDA), Palantir (PLTR), and Microsoft (MSFT), I believe there is an opportunity to indirectly participate in the growth of AI by investing in the infrastructure. For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the size of the data center market can increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 28.3% through 2030. After reviewing the details around some of NXG's top holdings, I was able to locate some positions that are directly aligned to service the growth in the market.

  • Constellation Energy: launched 'Crane Clean' energy center. Microsoft purchased energy from this renewed power plant to harness carbon-free energy for their data centers.
  • Vistra Corp: Agreement to support Meta Platforms (META) with its nuclear plants to help support operations, presumably for META's data centers.
  • Talen Energy: Previously sold a data center campus to Amazon (AMZN) to support their Amazon Web Services. This indicates the possibility that AMZN could eventually buy power directly from Talen.

According to data compiled by JLL Research, it is estimated that $3T in investment capital is required for 100 GW of new supply by 2030. This would indicate a CAGR of 14% through 2030. Someone has to service all of this demand, and NXG has direct equity exposure to many of the potential servicers. We are still in the early stages, so time will tell how it plays out, but the trend is clear as of now: large-cap companies leading the sector continue to heavily invest in their AI infrastructure.

Valuation & Performance Risk

At the time of my last coverage, NXG traded at a discount to NAV of 2.1%. However, the fund's valuation has recently increased to a small premium to NAV of 0.98%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see how NXG now trades at one of the highest price-to-NAV valuations over the last five years. Additionally, NXG continues to trade at a higher premium to NAV level compared to infrastructure peers. However, I believe that NXG's focus on modernized digital infrastructure justifies this slight premium, since demand and expectations around AI are high.

However, I want to emphasize that NXG's focus on income generation may not be the best approach for investors looking to maximize their total returns from the sector. If the estimated growth data is right, I believe that NXG will underperform more traditional energy-focused ETFs out there. Since NXG prioritizes income generation, the fund needs to actively sell off portions of the upside growth of its positions. This means that some of NXG's positions won't be able to experience uninterrupted growth during strong bullish momentum.

To demonstrate this weakness, we can refer to the fund's performance over the last five years. NXG's share price has increased by nearly 25%, while the total return actually sits above 113% when including all dividends that were paid out to shareholders. If we assume a similar structure going forward, investors should anticipate that approximately 80% of their total return will come from the fund's ability to sustain a high rate of distributions, while only the remaining 20% will actually come from the fund's price growth over time.

The focus on distributions can also be a detriment if market momentum shifts and the sector experiences a downturn. Since the fund may still pay out a large rate of distributions, NXG may find itself in a scenario where it is paying out more than it actually earned in income. So investors should not assume that a higher dividend yield automatically translates to better resilience during periods of market uncertainty. Additionally, the fund uses leverage in an effort to amplify its returns, which can increase its risk profile. The use of leverage can amplify downside risks if allocated to securities that do not perform as anticipated. Total borrowing of net assets sits at 31.05%, which is equivalent to roughly $129.9M.

Additionally, investors seeking to maximize total returns have historically been better off choosing a more straightforward ETF that tracks infrastructure holdings. The iShares U.S. Infrastructure ETF (IFRA) has exposure to some of the same underlying holdings and also tracks companies that may benefit from the expansion of AI and data centers. When comparing the performance, we can see that IFRA has significantly outperformed with a total return of 159.5%, despite having a much lower dividend yield of 1.7%.

Therefore, NXG may only be suitable for investors that specifically want to focus on income generation. This is typically the case for older or retired investors that want to create a supplemental stream of income to help support lifestyle expenses. If you are a younger investor who doesn't have the need for income at this time, you may have less money at the end of the road if you choose NXG.

Variable Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.54 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 12.8%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $6.48 per share. Unfortunately, there hasn't been an updated annual report released yet, so we can't review the fund's latest earnings details. However, I wanted to take a look at the older semi-annual report for 2025 to simply demonstrate the issues that such a large distribution has. For instance, here are the fund's annual earnings over the last few years:

  • 2020: ($7.85) per share
  • 2021: $9.94 per share
  • 2022: $4.06 per share
  • 2023: ($10.96) per share
  • 2024: $17.93 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): ($1.96) per share

As we can see, the fund's earnings are wildly sporadic year over year. The main issue is that net investment income is inconsistent and very low compared to the necessary level needed to support distributions. Therefore, the fund is almost entirely reliant on positive net realized gains to fuel NAV growth and support distributions. The challenge is that net realized gains can only be captured during periods of positive market momentum, which means that NXG has to actively sell off portions of its positions to lock in the gains. If the sector doesn't grow, there's a slim chance that NXG will be able to support its distributions. Therefore, I believe that payouts are overly generous at this time, and NXG would see a healthier level of price growth if payouts were reduced by about 20%.

A reduced dividend would allow the NAV to show a bit more growth during the stronger performing periods and reduce the negative impact of underperforming years. On the bright side, the fund has indicated that it can generate earnings that far exceed distributions when market conditions allow. For instance, the earnings for 2024 were strong enough to support several years' worth of distributions:

2024's earnings of $17.93 per share / $6.48 annual payout = 2.76 years.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on NXG to a buy. The fund is directly positioned to benefit from the growth of AI and data centers. NXG's modernized approach to infrastructure can help the fund generate attractive earnings in the near term, but there are some caveats. For instance, the fund's emphasis on income generation can translate to underperformance against more traditional ETFs that can capture price growth better. Although NXG is an income fund, the reporting indicates that the fund is heavily reliant on net realized gains to fuel its NAV growth and support payouts. So if the momentum of the sector shifts and the thesis is incorrect, NXG may find itself in a position where it is paying out more than it actually earns.

data.title: Commodities: Markets Poised For A Risk-Off Day
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy and Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Energy – European gas prices continue to rally

The oil market is under pressure in early-morning trading today, amid a broader risk-off move. President Trump threatened a 10% tariff on several European countries that oppose his Greenland plans. These tariffs would kick in on 1 February and increase to 25% on 1 June, until a deal for the US to acquire Greenland is reached. Reports suggest the EU is set to halt the EU-US trade deal and potentially revive a EUR93bn tariff package on US goods. There’s also a push from France for the EU to use its anti-coercion instrument against the US. This would restrict US access to the EU single market. There will likely be plenty of noise this week around these developments, particularly as both world and business leaders gather for the World Economic Forum in Davos.

Despite the pressure on flat price, the prompt ICE Brent timespread remains firm, suggesting some tightness in the spot physical market. If forecasts of a large surplus are correct, we should see spreads come under pressure, along with weakness in the flat price.

The latest positioning data shows that speculators increased their net long in ICE Brent by 85,496 lots over the last reporting week to 208,461 lots as of last Tuesday. This is the largest position held since September. This move was predominantly driven by fresh buying amid growing supply concerns from Iran, given the recent protests in the country and the potential for US intervention. Although these concerns have subsided in recent days.

European gas prices continued their rally on Friday. TTF settled more than 11% higher on the day. The market settled at EUR36.88/MWh, the highest close since June. EU gas storage is now just 50% full, well below the 5-year average of 65% full. Meanwhile, forecasts for colder-than-usual weather towards the end of January are proving bullish for prices. We had been warning for some time about the risk of an aggressive short-covering rally in the European gas market, given the record short position held by investment funds in TTF heading into winter. The rally in the European market has seen gas prices in the region trade at a premium to Asian LNG. This should attract additional LNG supply into Europe, helping to alleviate some of the growing tightness concerns.

Agriculture – Stronger-than-expected Asian cocoa grinding data

London cocoa prices settled 1.7% higher on Friday, recouping some of the losses from earlier in the week. The late-week market strength was driven by stronger-than-expected grinding data from Asia. Data from the Cocoa Association of Asia shows that cocoa grindings increased by 8.9% quarter-on-quarter to 197kt in the fourth quarter, while grindings were down 4.8% year-on-year. The market was expecting a larger YoY decline. This leaves total Asian grindings for 2025 at 770kt, down 9.1% YoY.

The latest CFTC data shows that money managers increased their net short in CBOT corn by 65,348 lots to 81,774 lots as of 13 January. The move was predominantly driven by a 43,230 lot increase in the gross short position. Meanwhile, for soybeans, the net speculative long fell by 44,756 lots to 12,961 lots (the least bullish since October 2025), with a large amount of fresh selling in the market. Finally, CBOT wheat saw a marginal change over the reporting week, with speculators decreasing their net short by 936 lots to 106,229 lots.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.
